does: This survey provides a comprehensive overview of methods for generating natural language concept descriptions of neural network components in large language models, including neurons, attention heads, and sparse autoencoder features. The authors catalog the key technical approaches that use generator models to produce open-vocabulary descriptions, document the landscape of evaluation metrics spanning both automated measures and human evaluation, and compile the datasets enabling this research area. The survey identifies the current state of the art while highlighting a critical gap: the field increasingly recognizes the need for more rigorous causal evaluation beyond existing descriptive metrics, establishing this as a central challenge for advancing interpretability work.
connects: This work builds on decades of neural network interpretability research that seeks to understand decision-making in deep learning systems. It extends earlier work on neuron-level analysis and attention visualization by leveraging recent advances in language models as powerful tools for generating human-readable explanations. The paper connects to the broader mechanistic interpretability agenda exemplified by work on circuit analysis and feature attribution. It also builds on parallel developments in sparse autoencoders as a method for extracting interpretable representations, and responds to growing interest in using LLMs themselves—via prompting and generation—as instruments for understanding other models rather than only as subjects of interpretation.
enables: This survey establishes foundations for developing more principled approaches to evaluating concept descriptions through causal methods that go beyond similarity metrics. It points toward future work creating standardized benchmarks for comparing description quality across different generation approaches. The roadmap enables research on using concept descriptions for concrete downstream tasks like model editing, bias detection, and safety verification. It suggests opportunities for tighter integration between concept discovery and model understanding, potentially allowing concept descriptions to guide mechanistic analysis rather than merely post-hoc summarize it. The work also opens questions about scaling these methods to larger models and developing more efficient generation approaches that maintain interpretability quality.
